title: 8.1 and 8.2 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 8.1 and 8.2!
topic_path: Causes of the First World War | 8.2

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was Archduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated in 1914?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Serbian nationalists wanted to resist Austro-Hungarian control and spark broader support from Russia.
The Ottoman Empire sought revenge for Serbia’s independence decades earlier.
Russian leaders hoped to weaken Austria-Hungary by initiating war through an assassination plot.
The Emperor of Austria-Hungary plotted the assassination due to personal tensions with Ferdinand.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how *imperialism* contributed to the expansion and escalation of World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Competing empires sought to gain advantages by seizing global resources and trade routes.
European countries fought to expand by taking land from one another across the continent.
The Central Powers aimed to take over the overseas colonies and resources of the Allied Powers.
The Allied Powers hoped to strip the Central Powers of their territory and global influence.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following supports the theory that the First World War happened by accident?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Governments were unable to maintain effective diplomacy as the crisis escalated too quickly.
Franz Ferdinand was killed by mistake, and the confusion led to war.
New communication technologies created confusion and misinformation that led to hasty decisions.
The Russian and Austro-Hungarian Empires stumbled into war due to a misunderstanding.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did industrialization influence the course of World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: New industrial technologies made war harder to reverse.
New industrial technologies improved diplomatic relations.
Technological advances allowed civilians to escape war zones more easily.
Modern industry led to better medical care and fewer war casualties.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did nationalism contribute to the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and the start of World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Serbian nationalists wanted to free Bosnian Serbs and saw Austria-Hungary as an obstacle.
Austria-Hungary promoted nationalism by encouraging independence for Bosnian Serbs.
German nationalists supported the assassination to provoke a European war.
Russian nationalists aimed to expand into the Balkans by targeting Austria-Hungary.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is a global conflict like World War I considered a “world war” instead of just a war?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It involved powerful nations from multiple continents and had global consequences.
It was fought entirely in Europe, but it was longer than in previous wars.
Every country in the world sent troops to fight in the war.
It was the first war to be declared by an international organization.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did motivations for fighting help turn World War I into a global war?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Countries and empires hoped to gain new colonies and protect old ones.
Most countries fought only out of loyalty to treaties, with no interest in territorial gains.
Nations joined the war mainly to test out new military technology in combat.
Some countries joined late in the war purely to gain influence during post war peace talks.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did colonization help transform a local conflict into a global war during World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A European dispute expanded as empires involved their colonies for troops and resources.
Colonized people were mostly isolated from the war and had little impact on its outcome.
Colonized regions invaded Europe to demand independence.
European colonies refused to fight, so Europe had to expand the war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was a significant *political* consequence of World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It led to the collapse of empires and the rise of new nations.
It permanently ended global military conflict and ushered in an era of peace.
It caused European countries to abandon their colonies and grant full independence.
The major empires remained mostly the same after the war.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What were some lasting consequences of the collapse of the Ottoman Empire after World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European powers redrew borders in the Middle East, creating tensions that still exist today.
The Ottoman Empire quickly reformed into the United Arab Republic, promoting regional unity.
The collapse ended all forms of European influence in the Middle East.
The end of the empire led to the rapid spread of democracy in the region.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following excerpt is known as the “war guilt clause” of the Treaty of Versailles (1919):

>Germany accepts the responsibility of Germany and her allies for causing all the loss and damage… as a consequence of the war imposed upon them by the aggression of Germany and her allies.


**How did the Treaty of Versailles, particularly the war guilt clause, contribute to rising tensions in the postwar period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It humiliated Germany by forcing it to accept full responsibility for the war and pay reparations.
It brought long-lasting peace to Europe by clearly assigning blame and discouraging aggression.
It created strong alliances between Germany and its former enemies to prevent future wars.
It stripped the German territory of its most valuable resources, leaving it vulnerable.

assessment_items.question_content:
The cartoon below illustrates one of the causes of World War I. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**How does this political cartoon illustrate how the alliance system contributed to the outbreak of World War I?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A black-and-white political cartoon shows a chain reaction of aggressive stances among seven European powers before World War I. At the front, a small figure labeled Servia is being grabbed by a taller man labeled Austria. Austria threatens, “If you make a move, I’ll—”. Behind them, a large man labeled Russia grabs Austria’s shoulder, saying, “If you hit that little feller, I’ll—”. Germany, beside Russia, threatens, “If you strike my friend, I’ll—”, raising his fist. In the background, France and England prepare to intervene, with France saying “If you hit him—” and England chiming in, “Hi there, if you chaps—”.
options.caption: “A Threatening Situation,” illustration by Nelson Harding, 1912. Public domain. [Enlarge image](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chain_of_Friendship_cartoon.jpg)

choices.content: It shows how a conflict between two countries quickly escalated because their allies felt obligated to get involved.
It shows that alliances prevented violence by forcing countries to negotiate peacefully.
It demonstrates how Serbia and Austria-Hungary worked together to keep other powers out of the war.
It highlights how Britain and France encouraged the conflict in order to seize German colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“I am a Yugoslav nationalist, aiming for the unification of all Yugoslavs, and I do not care what form of state, but it must be free from Austria.”

>—Gavrilo Princip, testimony after the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, 1914

**How does this quote help explain how nationalism contributed to the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and the start of World War I?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Nationalist movements sought to break away from large empires and create independent nations.
Nationalism caused Europeans to support the spread of democratic reforms across empires.
Nationalism convinced all European powers to support Austria-Hungary after the assassination.
Nationalism encouraged Austria-Hungary to grant independence to its minority groups.
